Just after seeing it. First off, it HAS to be seen on the big screen to really appreciate the aerobatics when they are in those jets. IT IS SUCH AN ADRENALINE RUSH and it's just so freaking COOL!!! Secondly, did I mention how cool it is. Thirdly, it builds on the original without relying too heavily on nostalgia. For me the movie is Mavericks and Rooster/Goose even though Goose isn't there physically his presence is felt in the movie in a lovely, not shove it down your throat nod to the original. I got weirdly emotional when they went on their mission - like had proper tears coming down my face which I wasn't expecting. The addition of Val Kilmer is also a great moment. Tom Cruise and Jennifer Connolly have great chemistry. And finally, as someone who would always go to the theatre every weekend before COVID, James Bond and Batman didn't get be back into the theatre but Maverick did and it was well worth the price of admission. It's a terrific move that I would highly recommend that you go see (on the big screen).

**Spoiler alert** 

I had the privilege of attending an early screening and sadly this movie has gone woke too. 

In one scene Slider makes a surprise appearance and Maverick revisits his famous, "slider... you stink" line. 

Slider than responds with,  "do you want to do something about that?"; then proceeds to lead Maverick to the showers while "take my breath away" cues in the background. 

While they're in the shower Iceman makes a appearance with his new wingman- Pivotman. 

The real shocker is when Jester shows up, who has transitioned into woman,  and screams, "Hey girls is this an private party,  Or can anyone join?" 

You can imagine the rest. 

Eventually the real Top Gun is exposed.

You know every once and awhile a movie hits the big screen that really lives up to the hype. I will not be putting spoilers into my review. This is one ride you need to experience for yourselves.
What I can add is Tom Cruise and the every so lovely Jennifer Connelly as always their acting was superb. The supporting / other stars were also believable and the story is well written. It is touching , and exciting! It is quite easy to feel as if you are right there.
This shouldn't surprise anyone with the likes of Jerry Bruckheimer who is tops himself. And Tom Cruise over the years has fine tuned his acting. He has become a true master of his trade and a real professional who likes things done to where the experience is top rate for those watching. 
I will admit I was sold on this movie along time ago. With Tom Cruise and then you add stars like Val Kilmer ( we love ya man! ), Jennifer Connelly , Ed Harris and you have a serious amount of Hollywood royalty. And I refuse to sell the other actors and actresses short. All the actors/actresses in this movie performed well above expectations! 
If you are expecting another movie like the original Top Gun then you will be in for a new and amazing experience. 

If you feel the need for speed. This is one you do not want to miss! 
I am giving this movie a 10 myself. Only because there isn't anything above a 10.

Btw, It is always nice to see Ed Harris. I am glad he was involved. He is just one of those rare actors. As for the rest of the cast. I see very bright futures in store for them. 
Btw , I am going back to watch it again. 
Oh and the fact that Tom Cruise is an accomplished pilot most certainly helped!

What a beast of a movie this is! Go see it in theatres, you will regret it if you don't . This is not only a love letter to aviation, this is a love letter to the fans of PROPER action movies. Forget Disney, Pixar, DC, this is how action movies should be done with minimal CGI. Tom Cruise does not disappoint  It's incredible that we get a sequel almost 40 years after the original and Cruise still rocks it. It's partly a trip down nostalgia lane, bringing you back to the eighties when the original came out. But it's damn relevant as well in these times. A beautiful movie that keeps you in awe in your seats for the whole duration. Thank you Mr. Cruise & everyone involved in this masterpiece. Seeing this movie on a big screen was one of my best experiences in a long, long time in theatres.
finally a movie without the woke bs, and it's incredible to watch.

Rating 11/10 👍👍

Storyline is meh.BUT the cinematography is out-of-this-world par excellence,considering this not even a sci-fi or fantasy film.

The sprinkling elements of drama and romance didn't cut it for me despite its attempt to retain the old school Hollywood charm from its prequel. 

The film could have delved into the intricacies of aircraft maintenance,for displaying the sophisticated behind-the-scenes kind of technical work would greatly complement the glory of flying a fighter jet on a mission, and this movie would be a timeless.

Overall,it's a great movie.

SPOILER: Movie was good, Ice talked with help of a computer screen, and dies in the movie from reoccurance of cancer.  The main plot is the adversary is on the verge of operating a new uranium plant.  Maverick was called by Ice to teach the latest Top Gun grads.  Goose’s son hates Mav.  They train and train, and when Ice died, Mav was pulled out if this mission.  But Mav “stole” his F18 to prove the mission can be accomplished below the standard alloted time.  They go on the mission, blows up the adversary’s secret plant.  Then they all had to do some fancy flying against SAMs and MIGs.  Mav’s plane was hit and he was presumably dead.  The remaining team flys home, except for Gooseks son (Rooster) who searched for him and saved Mav from certain death from enemy helicopter.  Rooster who disobeyed orders to return, shows up and blows up the helicopter while simultaneously being hit by a missle.  Now both Rooster and Mav are in enemy territory.  They found an old F14, started it up, and took off.  Fought off the first enemy but had to contend with the 2nd MIG.  Of which Hangman comes in from nowhere and splashes the MIG.  They all fly home onboard aircraft carrier safe.  Mav gets Penny Benjamin and they live happily ever after since that was Mav’s last mission by the Navy.  This storyline is very similar to Star Wars where the team had to blow up the Deathstar with its one vulnerability.  When the enemy was about to wipe out the main character,  only to be saved by a team mate (Han Solo).  Great scenes… definitely see it at the theater!

Just watched it, its good. VERY logical story line.  more over they did all the things they REALLY needed to do to make this work.  They brought in the original film main editors (who btw are truly responsible for the success of the original film because the way they cut the film and did audio pickups to make it work was NOT the story Tony Scott made as he only had an idea and not a solid story.    The film is certainly good,  but is it better than the original ....no..  but thats not to say its bad.  its not bad at all,  and its a proper sequel that shouldve been made decades ago.  Thankfully the logic of the story still hold weight even with Tom Cruise being much older.   On a ranking 1-10 ide give it an 8.   in alphabetic rankings.. B+.   Where the original film excells is its rollercoaster ride of a hard driving score along side fast paced visuals.   Also,  contrary to all the talk about real in camera footage,  in actuallity the original film has alot, and even more dynamic shots than this new film.  Really re-watch the original film and look for all the real aeronautic shots.. there is lots of them though majority of the pilot dialog stuff is on a little set with  rear projection .

I wasn't old enough to live through the 80's which was probably one of the best decades to be alive. Through the stories I heard this movie is and will always be a legendary icon for it's time. It's perfectly understandable that not many movies had ever come close to reaching the incredible success accomplished with the original from 1986.

Fast forward 36 Years today and I finally got the chance to experience this thriller on the big screen! It had me feeling goosebumps from the sounds of the jets to the feeling of the G forces which had me pushing back on the theater's recliner chair with each take off.

I honestly had low expectations coming in and was pleasantly surprised to find this sequel an even better experience when compared to the original which I love.

Shoutout to Tom Cruise for sticking with his passion for a lifetime. His work is nothing short of legendary and a true testament to the legacy he has worked his entire life to achieve.

It’s rare that I get excited for a movie event…or at least excited enough to write about it.  The year was 1986.  I was 15 years old.  I secured my very first job working a DOD military dependent internship at the AIMD jet shop assisting Navy in Cubi Point.  I was the “grease monkey” assistant to those tasked with the repair and maintenance of the TF-41 engine-- the powerplant of the legendary F14 Tomcat.  I was moved to choose this job based on events of that year.  Watching from my High School auditorium on April 14, 1986, President Ronald Reagan addressed the nation regarding the swift and decisive action against the terrorist regime of Khaddafi.  One month later, the original TOP GUN movie was released. Watching it in the theater on the US Naval Base in Subic Bay, together with my HS buddies…it was something else!  We were proud of our Navy roots and proud of our President for his assertiveness.  Proud of the US Servicemen and women who carried out justice and of our Navy families.  It was a different time, different place…a different age.  Tonight, 36 years later, I am excited to finally see the premiere of the sequel to my favorite movie of all time!

Ever since I was a little kid, I've been obsessed with military aviation. I've been playing combat flight sims for almost 10 years and finally last year mastered *that* maneuver Maverick used in the movie on IL-2 1946 with the YP-80. Top Gun, naturally, is a movie I just had to watch. The original is excellent. I just hope the new one lives up to it. From what I've seen, and heard from critics, this movie is going to be a smash hit. Even if it isn't, I'm still excited to go see it with my dad. My granddad was a ground crewman for the Enola Gay and changed her spark plugs the morning they took off for Hiroshima, so my dad and I will be sharing something my granddad would've loved to be a part of God rest his soul.

Meanwhile, in Ukraine...

TOM CRUISE FLYBOYS **
World War-I gave us the flyboys
Who flew by the seat of their pants.
Many would never return from war
While others survived by chance.

Their planes were mostly canvas and wood
Gasoline, bullets, bombs and poison gas.
Every pilot carried his own pistol
Wearing leathers, scarf and goggles of glass.

Aviators had no Parachutes
To escape their burning plane.
Many were forced to jump to their death
Or self-inflect a bullet to the brain.

Blimps where known as battleships of the sky
The roar of their engines gave reason for fear.
They flew so high they were hard to shoot down
Hiding above clouds till their targets drew near.

Tracer bullets for the first time were used
In the guns of airplanes to set blimps afire.
The skies became man’s highway of death
With duty and honor their driving desire.

How many flyboys have we lost since then
Those days of the Great War and more?
Where do we get such brave souls of chance
Who rise from the rest in the battles of war?

By Kansas City Poet Tom Zart

As a Top Door Gunner / Crew Chief often practicing shooting down fixed wing aircraft in simulated dog fights with Harrier, Broncos and sometimes A-4s before the Hornets, I do not think I would have liked being confined to cockpit seat with an O2 mask on in a pressurized cockpit in an F-18 Hornet. I worked at Northrop on the F-18 sub units in El Segundo where the other half was manufactured by McDonald Douglas in Saint Louis, Missouri shipped by underground rail.    I always wanted to test my skills though when at China Lake against the Northrop F-5 with the Russian Communist Star stenciled on it and its Russian reading newspaper pilot I would run across at the barracks. I probably got a lot more skill though from just playing the video game at the snack bar there of WWII bombers for a lot of points that I think I set a record there at that snack bar while eating the Cheese Quesadillas. I enjoyed teaching though unlike Maverick. I also wager that Maverick's favorite rank to play in such a role would have been Rear Admiral.  As a flying Psychological Terror Non Commissioned Officer, I can definitively state that taking on a pseudo name like "Maverick" is like wearing your psychology on your sleeve or a tattoo if shot down and captured. To be a Maverick you wouldn't therefor have the need for a constant high scbool reunion every sequel or so. I think this movie is going to be terrible. I hope I'm wrong, but I don't think I am. 

I'm 36 years old. Born in 1986, same year this movie came out. My parents actually saw this in theaters when my mother was pregnant with me. Top Gun has been my favorite movie for 36 years. And unfortunately, I cannot like this. This new one looks like it has none of the soul of the old one. 

Top Gun wasn't just a movie about fighter jets. It's a story about growing up. It's about a "snot nosed jockey", aptly named "Maverick", a young, talented but cocky fighter pilot who, while talented, gets into trouble because he likes to do things his own way.  His behavior creates mistakes, some with pretty serious consequences. He's forced to learn and adapt. And through the mentorship of others, his girl, his rival, his best friend, and the archetypical father figure (Viper), he learns how to become a good teammate, and a better pilot. It's a growing up story. It a story about learning to take responsibility, shoulder burden and loss, and grow into a reliable and disciplined man. 

I don't think this new movie is going to be good. I don't think it's going to have the heart and soul of the original. It's just going to be fan service references. Sure, the fighter jet scenes are probably going to kick ass. But that's about the only thing that lets you know it's Top Gun, is it's got fighter jets and Tom Cruise. Big whoop. 

I just don't see this as being anywhere close to the same. I hope I'm wrong, but to me it looks like the new writers missed the mark on what made Top Gun so beloved.

Wow. I remember when they were filming this where I worked. I used to work as a plane captain (three person that inspects the fighter jets and helps launch the pilots) at the base in Lemoore, CA and I remember seeing Tom Cruise walking through the hangar, though I didn't get to meet him as we were specifically told not to interact with the crew at all. I did, however, inspect the jets used in the film and that camera rig in the cockpit made it really difficult to inspect the seats. Still though, pretty cool to see a movie get made, at least partially. Of course, this was years ago (and, yes, Cruise did go up in the jets, though he sat in the backseat.)

Not looking good on continuity at all...

Warning: Long read of my guess of the plot based on my imagination and limited understanding of USN operations...satire involved, take with lots of salt...


*Chapter 1: China Lake*

Maverick hot dogs (again), buzzes the guard house in the *China Lake* scene shown in the previous trailer.

Everyone is amused except for the unfazed boss, RADM played by Ed Harris, (a nice nod to his role in The Right Stuff) to ground him.

The said RADM does look like he has more than 30 years of service though.

*Chapter 2: X years later* : Maverick is a civilian, flying his Mustang and doing whatever he wants.

COMPACFLT (Val Kilmer, mentioned / photo only) has a special mission which requires a group of aviator to be trained.

He sends Warlock ("They're called orders, Maverick") to recall Maverick to active duty. 

Warlock: Your reputation precedes you.
Maverick: I have to admit, I wasn't
expecting an invitation back.
Warlock: They're called orders, Maverick.

Scene switch, cue familar song, Maverick riding bike without PPE...

Scene switch again, Maverick arriving to meet COMNAVAIRPAC (Hamm aka Cylcone), who states flatly: "Let me be perfectly blunt: you were not my first choice..."

He will soon be introduced to the new generation of aviators...

*Chapter 3: Cue aviators shit talking in the O club* and introducing Rooster, the only one not in khakis, coming out of his truck. He's still thinking about his career options as a truck driver, just like his late father...

"Should have gotten out after 8, it's all Maverick's fault.." he mused silently..

Scene switch, Cyclone, Warlock gives briefing in open hanger, introduces Maverick...

Scene switch: First TRAINING sortie, multiple a/c single boogie..

Hangman and Co are aware of Maverick's reputation but underestimate him. 

Rooster is just pissed off at being an O-3 at 40 years old and not getting his Department head tour /screened for command because the movie release keeps getting delayed.

*It's all Maverick's fault.*

They learn the fine art of old school dog fighting and go on their mission.

*Chapter 4: The Mission*

The mission goes well initially (Warlock : no turning back now) but Rooster gets shot down in undefined enemy territory.

Mav ejects from a perfectly functional A/C and they find their way to old F-14 and somehow fly it with Rooster taking the RIO seat.

They evade the SU-57 somehow and manage to get feet wet.

Chapter 5 : Finale 

The ill-maintained Tomcat is on its last legs now and somehow manages to get near the CVN.

Story can have 2 endings: 

a) They suffer a compressor stall (unknown country bought the F-14A, remember?) induced ramp strike (think REVLON) and Maverick's RIO is killed in the ejection again. Cue funeral, missing man flyby and curtain roll...






b) They somehow manage to land to the amazement of everyone and everybody cheers.
Curtain roll.

How accurate is this? 
I guess we'll know when we know.

I hadn't saw the original for about 20 years and bought it on 4K disc to tide me over. I've got a 7.1 Atmos surround with 180 watts on the main channels, with a 120 inch screen and 4K projection (which actually looks and sounds better than the Cinema). The restoration on the disc with the new sound mix and picture upgrade, along with the many hundreds of movies I've watched over the years is one of the best things I've seen and heard in a long time. The sound separation between dialogue and score, the depth and richness of the of the audio overall, the deep and accurate bass levels, the colours and definition of the picture, it blew me away. Amazing how a movie of this age can do this.

Do you know who the real top gun pilots are in the western airforces? It's the operators in the AWACS planes (the big bulky planes with a giant disc on top). You see, all those weaving and dancing in the air doesn't actually happen in real life. How a real dogfight goes is that the AWACS find the enemy planes, then navigate the US fighters behind or other advantageous positions while their radars are turned off.  Then they fire AMRAAM missiles beyond visual range. It doesn't even need to have a radar lock. The AWACS direct those missiles in the direction of the enemy and when they are close enough, the onboard computer will activate and guide the missiles directly to the enemy. Mostly flairs are useless at this point. Usually, there are 2-3 missiles following every enemy's ass for a sure kill. 

Most of the time, the US fighter pilots never even see their enemies, even on their own radars. The AWACS navigators are the ones who will confirm the kill.

Glad I stumbled onto your commentary and channel. I'm a private pilot who grew up wanting to be a fighter pilot. At age 6 we had a hometown guy training as an AF pilot. He flew over my house low enough to see his face and did a slow roll right over the top of us. I was hooked but in high school developed a need for glasses so flying fighters was out. I did the next best thing as a young man and earned my private pilot certificate and have been flying Cessnas for 42 years. You and your fellow fighter pilots are the only men in this world that I envy. Looking forward to more of your videos... I've already checked out your helmet cam of a Thunderbirds flight. I've always thought the "slot" position would be fun to fly.

Big complaint: I can't stand the idea of the trainees having this super cocky attitude of "we're the best, who can possibly teach us anything?" and the hubris of "pfff, 2 against 1?! You kidding me?!" It's just SUUUUUUUUUUUCH hacky screenwriting. In a real life scenario, pilots of that level would have a tremendous amount of respect for their instructors. They know what it means to get where they are. Also, the movie explicitly states that Pete Mitchell's reputation precedes him, so they all would have known what a huge deal he was. The original movie did a great job of showing off the cockiness of the pilots while maintaining a tremendous level of respect for their instructors both as superior officers and as authorities in their field.

What I think the plot will be; in the decades since leaving Top Gun, Charlie has died. Maverick has gone into flying experimental aircraft. He hasn't lost his reckless attitude by buzzing people on the ground. After one nasty flight he is summoned before the Board where he thinks he's being court-martialed. Instead, he is asked to return to TOP GUN to train the new batch of 'Best of the best' aviators for a high stakes mission. There he finds the pilots, while the best, are also piss poor in discipline, lacking the skills in dog fighting because of an over reliance on computers to deal with any problems, or threats, instead of instincts. 

In their first head-to-head, Maverick takes on two of the new pilots and easily shows his skills vs them, taking them both down, thereby highlighting their flaws, defects that they refuse to admit to. This is where Goose's son steps up saying that he won't make the same mistake his father made, after witnessing Maverick's brash nature behind the stick. Maverick also meets a woman mentioned in the first movie, Peggy Benjamin, an admiral's daughter he buzzed before.

Maverick and the other pilots continue to butt heads during training, but gradually they learn and show that they do have what it takes by engaging, and 'shooting down' Maverick, perhaps even using his 'hit the airbrakes' maneuver against him. Feeling they are ready, the pilots are given their combat mission, a country in the East is making nuclear threats against their neighbors and need to have these 'facilities' destroyed. They can't send in drones because they need 'the best' due to the air defense systems in place.

Taking off, Maverick and his flight make it to the target area to find it a hornet's nest of surface-to-air missiles, anti-aircraft artillery, and then-MiGs, because you must have a dogfight in TOP GUN. After all, the purpose of Top Gun is to teach pilots how to dogfight. During the fight, one of the pilots is shot down. Not to worry, they get rescued by CSAR at the very end. Goose's son has his own Hornet and channel's his father in shooting down multiple MiGs. Maverick gets that fifth MiG to make himself an ace. The threat on the ground is destroyed and the remaining aircraft return to their carrier.

In the end, the need for TOP GUN is highlighted to instill in new pilots that, while technology advances, the need for dogfight training remains, and Maverick decides to stay with TOP GUN permanently this time along with his new wife, Peggy as they ride off into the sunset. Lastly, I think the funeral at the end is for Ice Man.

Oh yeah. Can't wait. This looks really good. Already psyched for it. 
I can't expect it to be as good as the original—not much ever is. But it'll be really good nonetheless. I just hope they don't spend most of the time talking about age differences between Maverick and the new class and 'that was then, this is now' BS 'this isn't your grandfather's Navy, etc.  like 'Expendables' and 'Indiana Jones 4' did. Truth is, life isn't that simple. Younger isn't always the better choice for the job. In fact, quite often it's not. Let me relate the following true story: 

First, let me say I have the utmost respect and admiration for the US Navy. They're some of the best out there. Bar none...almost ;) 

In the 80s, the USS JFK (CV-67) was on route for a port of call and a special event in Boston. Arriving early, they decided to challenge our sleepy little local base to a 'war game'. The objective: The carrier had to overtake and invade a small island off the coast known as 'No man's island'. 
We had to defend it. 

The cards were stacked against us to say the least. We had practically no lead time on this challenge and thought, to a man, that they'd roundly beat us. Still, we relished the challenge. This was the 80s, and for obvious reasons, our best A/C were on alert status and could not participate, nor could those pilots and crews who were on alert. 

Aboard their carrier were the latest aircraft in the Navy inventory with the most advanced radar and avionics. The pilots were all young and able including some graduates of Top Gun fighter school, cocky as all get out and extremely skilled and talented pilots. They made sure to inform of us this many times. ;)
They also had the benefit of E2C Hawkeye AWACs and all the state-of-the-art electronics aboard their carrier and on board the electronic jamming aircraft. 


By contrast, we had 15+ year old airplanes and 35+ year old pilots, many in their 40s, most of whom were only part-time. For many, their 'day job' was flying for the airlines. As for our aircraft inventory—less than half of what was ready to launch off the carrier—like our pilots, some of them were 'part-time' too, ;) as we often had to 'cannibalize' the parts off one plane to use on another ready to launch. This was the usual situation, not just for the 'war game'. . ;) So we were used to it. 

Also, to be fair, I should mention many of our 'old men' pilots had flown combat missions in Vietnam back in the day. 

Well, not only did the Navy fail to take the island, but our aircraft downed all of theirs in dogfights off the Cape—all of those that had time to take off, that is. If memory serves, we lost no aircraft to 'enemy' action. And if that wasn't bad enough....we sunk the carrier.  

After their embarrassing loss, they abruptly cancelled the remaining days of the 'war games' and sailed for Boston and enjoyed liberty and partying for the special event. Who could blame them? They didn't publicize this 'war game' at all. It was briefly mentioned in the Cape press with a line something to the effect, 'The carrier left early for Boston celebrations'. 

So, moral of the story is, never underestimate age or experience and the wisdom that comes from those. Youth is no match for experience.

Although I haven't seen it, I"ve heard that the new Matrix movie is hot garbage. With that said though, Ghostbusters Afterlife showed that beloved franchises can be handled well in the current age. If anyone feels the same way as I do, then here's my advice: be cautiously optimistic. Don't fall for the hype in case the movie turns out terrible. Top Gun is more than a movie. Is pop-culture icon. 
If you said at loud "Iceman" or "Maverick" people will associate it with those two characters respectively, even if you're talking of something entirely different. 
A good icon deserves praise, and... Well... So far, the movie looks amazing, but I'm concerned about some details.

The original premise of Top Gun was about a flying school for fighter pilots, with emphasis on dogfights, training them to use Heavy Fighters as Light Fighters on said combats... Before going forward, just think about the collosal size and weight of the iconic F-14 versus a lighter fighter, like the F-16 for example. With that in mind, it's safe to say that any pilot that can make that fat beauty move that fast it's an ace, by all means. 

Now, this movie will be about the same?  About the same idea for an elite group of pilots as experts on dogfights? 
Honestly... I hope so. However, the aircraft for the task is... Good, but not what I expected.

When the original Top Gun movie was out, the guys from Fightertown used the legendary F-14 Tomcat. A supersonic beast of variable geometry that was a show to see. In real life, the aircraft was amazing but very troublesome in so many aspects. So the Pentagon "axed" the aircraft in favor for a more, let's say, uncompromised aircraft, which is the F/A-18 Hornet. 

The Hornet, in contrast with the Tomcat, has better capabilities overall, except for terminal speed, but it is a good aircraft, and apparently easy and cheap to maintain (for military standards) since they been using it since the Cold War and there's no apparent full-time replacement for the ol' Hornet for the time being. 

Even if the Hornet aged decently and managed to win his own merits on the battlefield, I will say this plainly: I don't like it. 
It's something esthetic, nothing relevant, but if the movie keeps the premise of the classic Top Gun, then... I don't think is the right aircraft for the job. 
In comparison with Tomcat, from almost forty years ago, today's Super Hornet should be a vacation. So where's the challenge on using an ol' but updated and reliable fighter, instead of using something newer that still needs capable pilots?
True, a counter-argument can be that they want to make their pilots better using the Hornet as testbed before giving them better aircrafts, but I must say, in my experience as a pilot, that is... Not very effective. If you're going to train a pilot to flight, let's say, an F-35, you need to give that pilot said aircraft, not other stuff. The pilot needs to learn how the aircraft behaves, how it moves, how it reacts... Just like in the classic movie. The proposition of the program was about the Tomcat. Not any other fighter or aircraft on the Navy or the USAF catalog. Just the Tomcat.

It ticks my curiosity why they choose the Hornet and not other aircraft, like for example, the F-22 Raptor, which is still a killer fighter and one of my favorite aircrafts from all times. The tech of that aircraft is still unrivaled.
But, and as we see in the trailer, part of the movie apparently will be in an aircraft carrier, and the F-22 was never meant to take-off from a catapult, so... There goes that dream. 

If the Raptor is not available, then why not something even more "up-to-date" like the F-35? It was featured in several movies already, like Die Hard and The Avengers. Even it has carrier-friendly variants, and a VTOL variant... Yes, yes, I'm aware that the F-35 is kind of sketchy about his real capabilities and what we know so far is not that great (aside from their price tag and budget, which is huge) but by the end of the day, the reality is... This is a movie. It can take a few "realistic aspects" and totally forgot about those, if that means a better movie then I'm all up for it. 

My point is that, while the Hornet is a very good aircraft, the hard truth means it doesn't represent our current times where we seen plenty of new aircrafts. That's the plain and short.

Somehow, I think it will be a good movie. I just hope there will be a good reason why they're not using more up-to-date aircrafts.

How do you get a 55-year old man into the cockpit of a supersonic fighter plane?? I'm sorry, if Maverick had simply been an instructor on the ground, or an officer aboard an Aircraft Carrier, I would be able to understand that. But a fighter pilot, in his mid-50's?!!? I'm sorry, there's unbelievable, and then there's just stupid. There is no way the US Navy would be okay with Maverick still physically flying fighter planes. He would be deemed far too valuable an asset, as an officer, and let's face it. In real life... people in their 50's never fly anything faster than a 747! There is a reason for that. Life or death reasons. Not to mention the multi-million-dollar aircraft on the line.

I'll still go see this movie, because I loved the original, and so did my friends (who I'll be going with), and I anticipate it'll still be a good time to be had. But I'm extremely disappointed in the level of disbelief I am being required to suspend here. Also, yet again, it feels like Hollywood is just doing another sequel, rather than coming up with original stuff anymore! I'm sick of Hollywood LIVING IN THE PAST!!!
